sentences.text: Well, after I escaped from the children's nursery in Kennington Oval in London, I was brought to a lovely family in Sussex, in the country, which is south of London.
And the lady, Mrs.
Marjorie Rosevere, hugged me and kissed me.
And I was so afraid to be taken out of London, because I said, how will I escape?
How will I know where to run or where to go?
But after I met her, I was perfectly at ease.
And I was with them for three months.
They had a daughter my age.
Her name is Brenda.
And my foster-- they asked me to call them aunt and uncle, and I did.
And my uncle died at age 80 and my foster mother, Marjorie, is now 103 years old.
And I go to visit her every two years and sent them food packages all through the war.
We're very close.
And that's really my family.

And then they-- when their son was coming home from college, they needed the room and sent me to a cousin of theirs, Muriel Blakey, in a little village near [BACKROUND NOISE] the one I lived in, which was Copthorne.
And then I stayed with this other family.
And they took in seven evacuee childrens-- children out of London.
And I helped take care of them.
And there was a pair of twins.
And the little runt of the litter, Freddy, absolutely adored me.
And subsequently, years later, I saw them again when they were gray-haired men.
And I remember he came to us from London and he had a running ear.
He had an ear infection.
And after seeing him-- not seeing him for 30 years-- no, 40 years-- the first thing I said to him, how is your ear?
He says, you know, it still bothers me.
[LAUGHS] During this time, did you go to school or--
No.
I had private tutors.
They didn't send me to school.
I really don't know why they didn't.
I guess they couldn't.
But they sent me to lessons.
And I took shorthand lessons and painting lessons.
And I-- I was taught English watercolor methods.
And we went to concerts and I learned a lot about music.
And they corrected my English.
Every second word, I asked, how do you spell that?
So I never went to school, but I picked up English and became fluent and can write flawlessly, without ever having learned it at school just because they corrected me.
And they gave me fairy tales to read that I-- where I knew the story, and learned to read and write English that way.
Who supported you during this time, in terms of the food and--
Well, the-- the foster families fed me and housed me.
And I had a little money when-- that my parents sent.
Then my aunt and uncle in Israel sent me a little money, very little.
I'm really surprised that they were as stingy as they were.
[LAUGHS] They sent me very little money.
And I didn't need anything.
I really didn't need anything.
I had everything.
And I painted Christmas cards.
The people that I lived with, the Blakeys, were very devout and very religious in the Church of England.
And they had a women's club.
And they sold my Christmas cards that I-- what I didn't realize is this is at the height of the war and I was painting little Austrian boys and girls in lederhosen and that was the enemy.
But they still bought my Christmas cards that were hand-painted.
I would cut little windows out, like little Austrian cottages with flowerboxes and things like that.
And that's how I supported myself, make pocket money.
And I was teaching German to a few people.
I don't-- I mean, they were so kind.
This was during the war, mind you, with Germany the enemy, and they they did that for me.
So they were just wonderful.
I really renewed my faith in humanity after that.
Well, it affected me because the planes were overhead.
And we lived right near Gatwick Airport, which is now a-- a commercial airport.
At that time, it was an army airport.
And there were no bombings when I was there.
The major bombings and V- V-2 rockets came after I left.
My parents experienced all of that, but I didn't.
I was afraid, because from early childhood on, I always learned about gas wars and about poison gas and stuff like that, so I was deathly afraid of the war.
And-- but I didn't want to leave England.
I wanted to stay there.
But when my visa came through in November 1939, my parents insisted that I go to America, that I don't stay there, although I really didn't want to leave.
And that's when I left.
What I didn't tell you before was in Vienna, when Hitler marched in, the Nazis took over my grandparents' farm.
My grandmother was forced to leave the little town where they lived in her beautiful house, and moved in with my aunt and uncle in Vienna.
My grandmother, Theresa Kohn, and my aunt Camilla Kohn-- Grunwald.
And both of them subsequently died in concentration camp in Poland, in Lublin.
And I don't know what year.
I wrote to them.
Saying goodbye to my grandmother was also very hard.
And none of us really said very much.
I mean, they didn't say good luck or-- or anything.
We just kissed and cried.
And she put a bracelet on my arm that was a very beautiful bracelet with diamonds and rubies, which was subsequently stolen in New York-- I mean, in Chicago.
But it-- we didn't really need any words.
We-- we all knew what it was like and that we might never see each other again.

Of course, I didn't know that they would take that poor little old lady-- that poor little old lady was a year younger than I am now, but she was really an old lady-- and dragged her into concentration camp.
And I just hope that she died on the way.
My uncle, his name was Ignaz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Grunwald.
And he also was taken to concentration camp.
They had one son, who was a doctor and was able to get to America with his wife, with great difficulties.
And we had hoped that he would be able to get his parents out in time, but he-- he wasn't able to.
And my mother's brother fled to Uruguay.
And I thought that he could help my grandmother.
His name was Rudolf Kohn and changed his name to [?
Kuney. ?] And he didn't-- he wasn't able to bring my grandmother out.
I was able with-- at age 17 to bring out to my parents and a girlfriend from Vienna.
But my grown-up uncle and cousins couldn't do anything.
They tried, they said.
But they didn't try hard enough.
My constant striving was to help my parents.
There was not one moment of any day that I didn't think of how I could get them out.
And I didn't stop asking anybody and everybody, can you help my parents?
You said that you came out with a cousin?
No.
I came out with this Mr.
Jaretzki.
My mother's sister's son?
Yeah.
He came separately.
And I don't even know when he left or how.
That was after I left.
His name was Eric Grunwald, Eric.
And he changed it to Gordon.
And, you know, after I left and I was in England and the war broke out, I mean, we had total lapses.
I had no contact with any of my family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when I came to the United States, my parents were in internment camp.
So all those things are not as clear as they might be, and it's so long ago.
Right.
Well, we had 13-- we carried 13 subjects.
And we had professors.
And each period I guess was an hour long.
And you started school at 8:00 in the morning until 1:00 in the afternoon.
And we had religion, which was the first subject on the report card.
And because there were so few Jews, we had to go to a different school on Wednesday afternoon, where Jewish children came from all over Vienna and had religious instruction by a rabbi professor.
And we learned to read ancient Hebrew and Biblical history.
And we got a grade.
And if you didn't pass, you could flunk.
We also had to attend services once a week in a Jewish temple, and I mentioned that.
My favorite subjects were geography and history and German composition.
I enjoyed writing essays.
And we had physics and chemistry and all the subject that children have in high school here, except that we were that much younger.
We started at age 10.
High school was at age 10.
Never.
Not once.
Never.
You didn't ask, because you knew that if they helped you that they might be in danger.
There was one neighbor-- it was a peculiar building.
People lived there from the time they were born.
And there were a lot of artists living in that building.
We had a very famous sculptor by the name of Zelezny.
We had a painter of religious art by the name of Kempf.
And the emperor came to that building and we had a statue of Emperor Franz Josef I in the garden, because he came to visit the artists.
And it was a very famous building.
And one man who was a colonel in the Austrian army, and he walked very stiffly as if he had swallowed a broomstick, never spoke to us, because they were very anti-Semitic.
Never even greeted us and just walked past us from time immemorial.
And we just took it for granted that [NON-ENGLISH] just was like that.
When I left, when I was packing and the suitcases were outside our door, this stiff man walked past and started to talk to us for the first time.
Since my father was born in that building, this man had lived right above us and never spoken.
And he said, forgive us because we do not know what we do.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's what he said.
So that was the only should I say kind word that we ever experienced.
I was amazed, absolutely amazed.
We all were.
And subsequently, my parents told me that they always greeted them and said hello and good morning, when it was really dangerous to do these things.
I really can't recall anything that is of particular great interest, except that we were so busy trying to save our skin.
[BACKROUND NOISE] I mean, you're so busy, this is your total preoccupation all day long is, where can I walk that's not dangerous?
How can we get money out of the bank?
Our maid quit.
The help in the stores quit.
Nobody wanted to work for us anymore.
We were afraid that somebody would inform against us for some crazy reason.
I mean, you couldn't trust anyone.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what else to tell you.
That's really how it was when I was there.
That wasn't really my job.
That was my mother's job.
I was so busy with running around to different consulates and trying to get visas or getting out and writing letters and trying to learn English.
Oh.
And I forgot.
My mother enrolled me in a cooking school and in a hat factory, so that I should learn a profession when I left the country that I would know how to do some things.
And that's-- that's all we did.
And you know, at that age-- I was 16 years old-- what-- what does one really know?
How-- with all this happening, how did you feel being a Jew?
Well, I think I would have liked not to have been a Jew, of course.
It would have been easier not to.
I often ask myself that question.
I have come to the conclusion that I was happier being a victim than a persecutor, because I don't know what I would have done if I had not been a Jew, how I would have reacted.
And I might have been afraid of the reactions, because I felt that people who didn't actively hurt anybody and just looked on were just as guilty.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I felt that life was made easier for me being a victim.
Well, I left England on Armistice Day.
And leaving England during the war from Southampton harbor was not an easy matter.
The woman that I lived with, this Muriel Blakey, took me by train to Southampton.
And it was one of these terribly foggy days that you only see in a Sherlock Holmes movie.
You couldn't see your hand in front of your face.
We got to Southampton and there was-- you just couldn't see anything.
And I had to get on a train and leave my friend and say goodbye to her.
And in the excitement, she was holding my raincoat and I didn't have a coat.
[LAUGHS] I left that behind.
I got on the train, the boat train to the harbor.
And there I got off the train and got on to a tender somehow, in this thick fog.
And suddenly, out of the fog rose this great big hull of a gray ship.
And we had to go up on a rope ladder.
And that was very difficult, the rope ladder was swaying and then the ocean below and it was really scary.
And we got up on the ship and there were guns all over and sandbags all over.
And I got into my cabin and it was a cabin that was as big as my living room here.
It was enormous and very beautiful.
And it was a very luxurious-looking ship inside.
And [SIGHS] a person knocked on my door and he said, Miss Harmel, he said, we would like you to sell poppies on the ship, Armistice Day poppies.
And I says, oh, I would be delighted.
And he said, you're English, aren't you?
I said, no, I'm not.
I'm stateless, because my passport had expired and I didn't want to get a German passport, so I refused to go to the German consulate to renew my passport.
And the British gave me a certificate of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the correct wording.
But it was a-- a certificate that I was stateless and that I was leaving Britain in good standing and I was coming to America.
So when he found out that I was not English, I couldn't sell poppies, [LAUGHS] but-- because we were at war at the time with Germany.
So then there were tremendous storms.
It was November and the storms were tremendous.
And I always felt the ship was going to break in half.
And like I said, mines had been swept loose and U-boats were attacking and-- and we could hear the guns going off.
But they told us it was target practice.
In the middle of the ocean, it was target practice.
That's hard to tell, because it was so stormy most of them were sick, so nobody came to the dining room.
I was in the dining room alone.
But it was a big ship.
It was 26,000 tons.
And it sank shortly thereafter on one of the trips.
Going back, you see, it was carrying arms from America to France.
And it was sunk.
Muriel Blakey.
That was the cousin of the Roseveres' that I was living with.
I was 18.
And I arrived at the end of November, 1939.
I explained to you that my parents had bought the passage in Vienna on the And the French Line eventually made good and gave me the passage on the All I was allowed to take out of England was $5.
And I came with $5 in my purse.
And with that, I brought-- bought roses for my telephone book aunt in New York.
Well, I had all those formals [LAUGHS] and underwear.
The first place that I went was the telephone book Harmels from New York.
They had two sons.
And one is Dr.
Merril Harmel, who is a famous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anesthesiologist in-- at Duke University, now retired.
And his brother Richard Harmel, who just died very recently.
And his parents, Lou and Hermine Harmel.
They were not related, really.
They were-- I found them in the telephone book.
And they met me at the dock when I arrived, except-- they didn't meet me at the dock.
I had sent a telegram that I was arriving, but the telegram was censored so they cut out the date.
And so they didn't know when I would arrive and nobody gave them the information.
So when I-- when the ship docked-- well, first I want to tell you it was again very foggy when we passed the Statue of Liberty.
And that was so thrilling, you know, to see the Statue of Liberty.
Everybody was on deck at that time.
And when we arrived in New York harbor, all kinds of reporters came on deck from the newspapers.
And there were articles.
And there were even articles in Chicago about that I arrived, because it was a ship chased by submarines all the way across.
And everybody had somebody waiting for them at the ship except me.
And I was there 'til about 7:00 o'clock at night and nobody came.
And I'm sitting there with seven suitcases alone in the dark at the dock.
And nobody came.
And I finally found the phone number of my uncle in one of my suitcases and called.
And they lived in Brooklyn and I had to wait another hour before he and his son Merril came to pick me up.
And they came in that great big car.
And the radio was playing in the car and it was Toscanini conducting the and we went over the Brooklyn Bridge and it was so beautiful.
And I stayed with them for three weeks.
And then my telephone book uncle from Chicago was in New York on business.
And I went back by train with him to live in Chicago.
And their name was Schneider, Benjamin Schneider.
And I lived with them for several months until I found a place of my own and-- and a job.
And--
I was an apprentice to a dress designer at a dress factory in Chicago.
And it was my aim to become a dress designer, because I liked to draw and I thought all I would have to do is draw pretty pictures.
What I didn't realize is that a dress designer had to make patterns and be a dressmaker.
And I didn't like that part of it.
And that's where I met my first husband, who was a salesman.
And--
His name is Charles Rubin.
And I was married to him for 28 years and we had a wonderful daughter.
And the way I met him was the man I worked for-- of course, I asked him, would you give me affidavits for my parents?
That was my constant question.
I asked everybody I met.
He says, no.
He says, I'm dead broke.
I can hardly pay my bills.
He says, but I know a bachelor who is a very nice person, and maybe he'll do it for you.
So he set up a lunch date for me with Charlie.
And he was 34 years old at the time and I was 18.
And we had lunch and I told him my problem, that I needed someone to guarantee for my parents.
And he said he'll be glad to do it, so we went to the HIAS, and-- the Hebrew aid society, to make out the papers for my parents.
Well, they found out that he didn't make enough money and didn't have enough money, because all he owned was $800 and a car that he was making payments on.
So they said, you can't guarantee for two people, except if you were married.
So I said to him, would you marry me?
He said, I won't hold you to it, you know.
We'll just get married and I'll pay for that and then when my parents come over, we get divorced.
So-- this was the first day I met him.
And I said, you know, it'll only be pro forma.
It's just a legal thing.
And there was a movie after that, long time afterwards, you know?
That's really my story.
And he said, all my life I've been wanting to marry a girl like you.
He said, of course we'll do it.
So-- and subsequently we got married.
And it was a very good marriage.
And my parents came over after my daughter was nine months old.
They came over in 1943 on a convoy with 21 ships, 12 of which were sunk.
And Churchill was making a speech at that time, saying, as I'm speaking, a convoy is being attacked in the Atlantic.
And I just knew that was the convoy my parents were on, because they had sent a letter, we're leaving soon.
And I knew that that meant that they were leaving.
So that was very exciting, when they finally came and were alive and called from New York and came to Chicago and I had a nine-month-old baby girl.
My daughter's name is Diana.
Yes.
I went to the Art Institute of Chicago.
I was married and I went to school.
And I never graduated, unfortunately, because we moved.
At that time, it was difficult to get apartments and all that, so we moved to a suburb.
I went there for two years on and off, and then subsequently took lots of courses in art and art history.
OK.
